bounded_excerpt: 2 Guideline Resource Unit Last revision: November 2025 Background For gynecological cancers treated with a combination of external beam radiation and brachytherapy, especially cervical and vaginal cancers when high dose radiotherapy is given with curative- intent, adjacent normal vaginal tissue may be exposed to a high dose of radiation. Typically, the apex of the vagina can tolerate higher doses of radiation than the rest of the vagina. Tolerance of the lateral apical vagina can be as high as 140 Gy.1 Higher radiation doses are thought to contribute to late complications such as vaginal stenosis and shortening from fibrosis, in addition to ulceration which may manifest as dyspareunia and pelvic and vaginal pain.2 Radiation initially causes damage to the endothelium and ultimately causes progressive endarteritis of the small blood vessels resulting in cellular hypoxia and damage to the fibroblasts.3 This inhibits the ability of the irradiated tissue to repair itself and can result in non- healing ulcers. A radionecrotic wound gradually progresses to involve surrounding tissue and can result in vesicovaginal, colovaginal, and rectovaginal fistulae.4 Other manifestations may range fr

bounded_excerpt: 3 Guideline Resource Unit Last revision: November 2025 High dose vaginal brachytherapy is relatively safe. Kuren et al reported that a maximum surface dose of HDR brachytherapy dose of 103 Gy and the maximum cBED (cumulative biologically effective dose) of 878.6 Gy3 were not associated with fistula or necrosis or other grade 3– 4 vaginal complications. 2 The EMBRACE I study was an observational study from 2008 -2015 of 1,416 patients with locally advanced cervix cancer. MRI guided 3D treatment planning was the primary imaging modality with treatment based on Dose Volume Histograms. The 5- year risk of Grade 3 GI, GU events, vaginal stenosis, and fistulas was 8.5%, 6.8%, 21%,3.2% respectively.6 The EMBRACE II study (2016 onwards) used dose constraints for organs at risk.6 Additionally, it used advanced techniques such as IMRT. Early results from ESTRO 2025 show a decrease in grade 3 GI events and vaginal stenosis 1.7% and 14% respectively. This may allow for the use of volumetric, dosimetry and clinical risk factors to decrease complications.6 The PORTEC -2 study reported 2- 5% late vaginal toxicity grade > 2 or more) , with 20- 30% vaginal shortening or stenosis and sexual dysfunct

This may allow for the use of volumetric, dosimetry and clinical risk factors to decrease complications.6 The PORTEC -2 study reported 2- 5% late vaginal toxicity grade > 2 or more) , with 20- 30% vaginal shortening or stenosis and sexual dysfunct

bounded_excerpt: 4 Guideline Resource Unit Last revision: November 2025 Radiation proctitis treatment includes anti -inflammatory drugs such as 5- ASA, sucralfate and steroid enemas may help. Argon plasma coagulation (APC) can be used for active bleeding but may be complicated by rectal ulcers and fistulae. Most surgical repairs of fistulae related to radiation necrosis are technically difficult, due to a compromised blood supply with limited success with the use of myocutaneous flaps and skin. Rectovaginal and vesicovaginal fistulas are more common than rect ovesical fistulas 11 Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT) is a treatment modality which can repair radiation induced vascular changes. Transcutaneous oxygen measurements four years after HBOT have revealed near -normal levels, implying that angiogenesis effects are essentially perm anent. In fact, HBOT is the first available treatment for delayed radiation injuries that is potentially disease modifying, and is associated with healing in ulcerated, necrotic tissues.12 In April 2005, the Undersea and Hyperbaric Medical Society approved HBOT as effective treatment for delayed radiation injury. That same year, the Food and Drug Administration (U.S.) a

bounded_excerpt: 5 Guideline Resource Unit Last revision: November 2025 For radiation - induced pelvic soft tissue injury where it is expected that the lesion will remain chronic or deteriorate further, the treatment protocol for HBOT often consists of once- daily treatments at 2.5 atmospheres absolute pressure (ATA) on a five- days-per-week basis for up to 40 treatments, depending upon the patient’s condition. Follow -up should be at four to six weeks.15 For the treatment and prevention of osteoradionecrosis, a protocol by Marx, et al. consists of 20 HBOT sessions prior to surgery in previously irradiated area and 10 sessions afterwards.17 Each session involves breathing pure oxygen at 2.4 ATA for 90 minutes, but can vary between 2.0 and 2.5 ATA and between 60 and 120 minutes, as well as the frequency per day.18 Repeated HBOT sessions may be of benefit in refractory osteonecrosis. However, this may increase the incidence of middle ear barotrauma and sinus pressure. Treatment for these potential complications includes the use of nasal decongestants and prophylacti c myringotomies. Hence, it is important that patients on HBOT be monitored with tympanometry and otoscopy. Temporary vision changes, sei

bounded_excerpt: 6 Guideline Resource Unit Last revision: November 2025 Guideline Questions 1. Is hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT) effective in treating late radiation tissue injury with respect to soft tissue necrosis, cystitis, proctitis, bone necrosis, and other complications in women undergoing radiation for gynecological malignancies? If so, wh en should HBOT be applied and what dosing regimen should be used? 2. Should HBOT be used in combination with any other treatments?

bounded_excerpt: Search Strategy The PubMed database was searched for relevant studies, guidelines and consensus documen ts published up to July 2025. The evidence tables are available upon request. Online resources from oncology -based health organizations and guideline developers were also systematically searched, and relevant guidelines from the following organizations were considered in the development of our recommendations: European Society for Medical Oncology (ESMO), American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO), BC Cancer (BCC), and the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN).

bounded_excerpt: Target Population The following recommendations apply to adult cancer patients with gynecological cancer.
quoted_span: Target Population The following recommendations apply to adult cancer patients with gynecological cancer.
quoted_text: Target Population The following recommendations apply to adult cancer patients with gynecological cancer.

bounded_excerpt: Recommendations HBOT is effective for late radiation tissue injury in gynecologic mali gnancies. There is evidence for symptomatic benefit for certain clinical settings: following RT for cervical and vaginal cancer, radiation cystitis, soft tissue necrosis, or osteonecrosis, colovesical, colovaginal and vesicovaginal fistulas. The small number of case series and patient numbers make specific recommendations difficult. 1. Earlier HBOT may lead to better results before irreversible ischemia and fibrosis. 2. HBOT should be considered for women who fail conservative care. There may be a role for repeating HBOT in conditions which are thought to be refractory.

bounded_excerpt: 4. Outcomes may be better with 70 sessions rather than 30- 40. Discussion The HORTIS IV trial, published in 2008, reported improvement in QoL and improved symptom control in radiation induced proctitis. Only 14 of the 120 patients had completed follow -up assessments at 5 years.22 Oliai et al, in 2012, reported on 19 patients treated with HBOT for radiation in hemorrhaigc cystitis and proctitis. The mean LENT -SOMA score was reduced from 0.78 to 0.20 in patients with HC and from 0.66 to 0.26 in patients with proctitis. Complete resolution of hematuria was seen in 9 patients and partial response in 2 patients. Recurrence of hematuria occurred in 4 patients after a median of 10 months. Complete resolution of hematochezia was seen in 2 (50% ), partial response in 1 (25%), and refractory bleeding in 1 (25%).They concluded that HBOT had a role in cystitis and proctitis. 23

bounded_excerpt: Tahir el al, in 2015, reported on the safety and efficacy of HBOT in 278 patients. Only 68% had follow- up data. The overall response rate for osteonecrosis of the mandible, soft tissue necrosis of the head and neck and xerostomia was86%,85% and 64% respectively. Response rates for soft tissue necrosis at other sites, chronic radiation proctitis and hemorrha gic cystitis were 84%, 94% and 85%, respectively. Ear Barotrauma, reversible ocular toxicity, dental complications and myocardial infarction were 15.9 %,10.6%,4.2% and 0.5% respectively. They concluded that there was a role for HBOT in LRTI.24 Glover et al, in 2016 , published the HOT 2 trial. This was a randomized, double- blind, controlled phase 3 trial of HBOT for chronic bowel dysfunction after pelvic radiotherapy. 84 patients participated from 2009 to 2019. There were 12 patients who were followed up. They conclu ded that HBOT did not improve symptoms of bowel dysfunction including diarrhea and rectal bleeding25 Cardinal et al, in 2018 , published a review of HBOT in radiation induced hemorrhagic cystitis (HRC). Of the 602 patients that received HBOT for HRC, 84% had a partial or complete resolution. 75% of patients saw an

bounded_excerpt: 8 Guideline Resource Unit Last revision: November 2025 had documented follow -up. Of these, 14% experienced recurrence, with a median time to recurrence of 10 months (6 to 16.5 months). They concluded that there was a role for HBOT for HRC.26 Subsequently, in 2020, a meta- analysis by Yuan et al on the effects of HBOT on gastrointestinal complications of rectal bleeding, pain and diarrhea until 2019. Patients had previously been treated for cancers of the uterus, cervix, rectum and prostate. They noted an improvement in rectal bleeding (0.81,95% CI:0.74- 0.89) , diarrhea (0.75, 95% CI:0.61- 0.90) in pain (0.58, 95% CI: 0.38– 0.79).27 Andren and Bennett reported on an observational study of 52 patients previously treated for cancer of the bowel, bladder, cervix, prostate, and vulva, in 2020. They concluded that there was a role for HBOT in radiation cystitis and radiation proctitis.28 Giao -Lima et al, reported on the role of HBOT in the treatment of radiation lesions in 2022. This was a retrospective analysis of 88 patients, 33 with radiation cystitis, 20 with radiation proctitis, 13 with osteoradionecrosis of the mandible and 22 with radiation enteritis. bounded_excerpt: 9 Guideline Resource Unit Last revision: November 2025 HBOT at 12 months (mean difference (MD) - 10.72, 95% CI - 18.97 to - 2.47). There was a temporary higher incidence of loss of visual acuity and barotrauma. The application of HBOT to selected patients and tissues may therefore be justified. Further research i s necessary to establish the optimum patient selection, timing of any therapy and an economic evaluation. There is no specific information from this review regarding the efficacy or effectiveness of HBOT for other tissues. There was no level 1 evidence to recommend HBOT for women with late radiation tissue injury after undergoing radiation for cervical and vaginal cancer; this area has not been studied. Since the mechanism of action of late radiation tissue injury is thought to be similar following radiation treatment of cervical and vaginal cancer and radiation treatment elsewhere in the body. There is currently no high- level evidence or agreement as to the most appropriate dosing regimen. Oscarsson et al, recently reported on the 5 - year follow up with the use of HBOC in radiation cystitis, in 2025. 32 39 of 70 patients (56%) were eligible for assessment for 5 years. T

bounded_excerpt: 10 Guideline Resource Unit Last revision: November 2025 Regarding potential risks and side effects associated with the use of HBOT, it is advisable to have all potential candidates evaluated by a CPSA guideline compliant i hyperbaric physician to determine “fitness to dive” (i.e. assess treatment associated risks). Of particular concern are those patients who present with any of the following: middle or inner ear disorders, congenital heart disease, claustrophobia, congenit al spherocytosis, diabetes, seizures, lung problems, asthma, upper respiratory infections, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, eustachian tube disfunction, high fever, cataracts, pregnancy, or use of a pacemaker or epidural pain pump. In addition, the following are absolute contraindications to the use of HBOT: untreated pneumothorax and concurrent use of bleomycin, cisplatin, disulfiram, doxorubicin, and sulfamylon. 36 Health Canada must provide a license before any hyperbaric chamber is imported or sold in Canada. Under the Medical Devices Regulations, all medical devices imported or sold in Canada must be safe, effective, and of quality manufacture. 36 To ensure safety, the device is reviewed prior to lice

bounded_excerpt: 11 Guideline Resource Unit Last revision: November 2025 Proposed Intervention In HBOT, patients receive 100% oxygen inside a pressurized treatment chamber. Treatments typically involve breathing 100% oxygen, with pressurization of between 2.0 and 2.5 atmosphere absolute (ATA) (203 to 253 kilopascal (kPa) for between 60 and 120 minutes once or twice daily for a total of 30 to 60 sessions. The aim of HBOT is to increase the number of blood vessels in irradiated tissue, improve tissue quality, promote healing and prevent breakdown of irradiated areas .18

bounded_excerpt: 13 Guideline Resource Unit Last revision: November 2025 Development and Revision History This guideline was reviewed and endorsed by the Alberta Provincial Gyne cologic Oncology Tumour Team. Members include gynecologic oncologists, radiation oncologists, medical oncologists, nurses, pathologists, and pharmacists. Evidence was selected and reviewed by a working group comprised of members from the Alberta Provincial Gyne cologic Oncology Tumour Team, external participants identified by the Working Group Lead, and a methodolog ist from the Guideline Resource Unit. A detailed description of the methodology followed during the guideline development process can be found in the Guideline Resource Unit Handbook. T his guideline was originally developed in 2009 and updated in 2025. L evels of Evidence I Evidence from at least one large randomized, controlled trial of good methodological quality (low potential for bias) or meta-analyses of well -conducted randomized trials without heterogeneity II Small randomized trials or large randomized trials with a suspicion of bias (lower methodological quality) or meta -analyses of such trials or of trials with demonstrated heterogeneity III Prospect
